prompt: I've uploaded the Section J workload attachment for an Air Force base operations contract. The spreadsheet has two tabs: 'Sites' (139 rows, columns: Base Name, State/Country, DODAAC, AOR, CONUS/OCONUS) and 'CM History' (139 rows, columns: Base Name, DODAAC, FY2021 CMs, FY2022 CMs, FY2023 CMs, CM Average, footnote saying '28% of CMs resolved by phone without technician dispatch'). Analyze this workload data and tell me where my biggest pricing risks are.
expected_behaviors: Runs Workflow 0 first — classifies 'Sites' as Geographic Scope and 'CM History' as Demand/Volume History
Identifies DODAAC or Base Name as the join key between the two sheets
Extracts the phone-resolution factor (28%) explicitly, applies it to compute field-touch CM volume
Computes CAGR across FY2021-FY2023 and projects option-year volumes
Runs Pareto to identify which bases drive the most field-touch CMs
Flags OCONUS bases in the scope as pricing risk items
Notes that the phone-resolution factor is a variable assumption and flags it as a risk
Produces a ranked pricing risk list
Emits or offers to emit the PTW handoff JSON envelope
should_not: Assume specific column names without first reading them from the data
Present only raw CM totals without applying the phone-resolution adjustment

prompt: Here's the workload data for an IT help desk contract covering 12 regional offices. The data has one sheet called 'Ticket Volume' with columns: Office, Region, Country, Q1-2023, Q2-2023, Q3-2023, Q4-2023, Q1-2024, Q2-2024, YTD_2024_Total. Four offices are in Germany, two in Japan, one in Bahrain. No footnotes. Help me understand what this data means for pricing option years.
expected_behaviors: Runs Workflow 0 — classifies 'Ticket Volume' as Demand/Volume History with a geographic dimension embedded in it
Recognizes quarterly time series (6 quarters) and aggregates to annual equivalent
Computes YoY growth rate and flags that 6 quarters of data gives a moderate-confidence trend
Identifies Germany (4 offices), Japan (2 offices), Bahrain (1 office) as OCONUS and applies OCONUS risk tiering
Runs Pareto on ticket volume to find which offices drive concentration
Projects option-year ticket volumes with conservative/base/optimistic scenarios
Notes absence of adjustment factors (no footnotes) and does not fabricate any
Flags Bahrain as higher-risk OCONUS tier than Germany or Japan
should_not: Invent adjustment factors that aren't in the data
Treat quarterly data as annual without aggregating
Ignore the geographic dimension when discussing pricing risks

prompt: I have the Section J pricing attachment for a facilities maintenance contract. There are three tabs: 'Task Orders' (columns: TO Number, Description, Unit, Base Qty, Opt1 Qty, Opt2 Qty, Opt3 Qty, Opt4 Qty), 'Locations' (columns: Site ID, Installation, City, State, Zone), and 'Historical Activity' (columns: Site ID, TO Number, FY2020 Units, FY2021 Units, FY2022 Units, FY2023 Units). The base year quantities in Task Orders are flat across all option years. Historical Activity shows 6-12% growth each year for most task orders. What are the risks?
expected_behaviors: Runs Workflow 0 — classifies 'Task Orders' as CLIN/Task Structure, 'Locations' as Geographic Scope, 'Historical Activity' as Demand/Volume History
Identifies Site ID and TO Number as the multi-key join between sheets
Detects the critical mismatch: flat CLIN quantities vs. 6-12% historical growth
Computes what the gap will be in Option Years 3-4 if growth continues
Runs CAGR on Historical Activity and projects forward
Flags the flat-quantity FFP CLIN structure as a pricing trap — contractor bears volume risk
Produces ranked pricing risks with the quantity-gap as Rank 1
Includes estimator assumption to validate: 'Confirm with PM whether flat quantities reflect expected demand stabilization or an oversight'
should_not: Miss the flat-quantity vs. growing-demand contradiction
Analyze each sheet in isolation without cross-referencing
Fail to flag the contract-type risk (FFP with variable demand)
